intro: Martin Luther (1483–1546), German monk, theologian, and the central figure of the Protestant Reformation, was professor of biblical theology at the University of Wittenberg. His posting of the Ninety-Five Theses in 1517 ignited the Reformation, and his recovery of justification by faith alone — drawn above all from Romans and Galatians — reshaped Western Christianity. Luther was first and foremost an expositor of Scripture: he lectured through the Psalms, Romans, Galatians, and Hebrews early in his career, and his translation of the Bible into German (New Testament 1522; complete Bible 1534) set the standard for the German language and put the text in the hands of ordinary readers. His Lectures on Galatians (1535) — a greatly expanded reworking of his 1519 commentary — is his best-known exegetical work, prizing the distinction between law and gospel and the freedom of the Christian conscience; it is the source drawn on in the Cloud of Witnesses layer here. Long since in the public domain, Luther's commentaries and sermons remain foundational to Lutheran and broader Protestant biblical interpretation.
